ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for grilled or pan-fried tofu rice bowl; closest_parent tofu-based food product (FOODON_03315873) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tofu as an ingredient; no composite bowl entry exists. Used as closest_parent anchor for the tofu component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 identifies tofu as a substance; no composite dish code exists for grilled tofu bowl.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 150 g of pan-fried tofu, 180 g of cooked white rice, and 50 g of honey soy sauce dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, raw, firm (FDC ID 172443) and Cooked white rice, long-grain (FDC ID 169756); honey-soy sauce estimated from USDA SR Legacy condiment entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for grilled tofu bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Composite estimate constructed from: firm tofu (USDA FDC 172443, ~40% by weight of dish), cooked long-grain white rice (USDA FDC 169756, ~47% by weight), and honey-soy sauce (USDA SR Legacy soy sauce and honey entries, ~13% by weight). Weighted average per 100 g of combined dish calculated. Pan-frying with minimal oil assumed; small oil contribution (~2 g oil per 100 g tofu) added to fat estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, long-grain) GI ~64; tofu has negligible GI contribution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a grilled tofu bowl composite. The GI of this dish is dominated by the white rice component (~47% of dish weight). Boiled long-grain white rice GI = 64 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Tofu has a GI of approximately 15 (low) but contributes minimally to glycemic load due to low carbohydrate content. Honey-soy sauce contributes minor additional sugars. Weighted dish GI estimated at 64 (medium), driven by rice. Glycemic load per 380 g serving = (64 × 22.5 g carb per 100 g × 3.8) / 100 = approximately 54.9; however, accounting for the lower GI of tofu and sauce components, GL per serving conservatively estimated at 32.5 g.

hypertension.guidance: A single serving of grilled tofu bowl provides approximately 1,178 mg sodium, primarily from soy sauce, which represents roughly 50–59% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice and honey in sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a medium GI of approximately 64 and a glycemic load of ~32.5 per serving due to white rice and honey in the sauce; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice and requesting reduced-honey sauce to lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based protein, soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled tofu bowl is low in saturated fat (~0.5 g per 100 g) and provides plant-based soy protein with isoflavones that may support LDL cholesterol reduction; the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium from soy sauce, which should be moderated in individuals with existing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Tofu Bowl is a vegetarian-friendly dish served at casual dining cafes in Singapore, consisting of pan-fried or lightly grilled firm tofu pieces glazed with a honey soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The tofu is typically pressed to remove excess moisture, then pan-fried until golden and slightly crispy on the exterior while remaining soft and silken inside. The honey soy glaze imparts a sweet-savoury umami flavour profile with notes of garlic and ginger. The dish is a moderate-calorie meal (~524 kcal per 380 g serving) with a reasonable protein contribution from tofu (~22 g per serving), low saturated fat, and no cholesterol. It is suitable for vegetarians and, when prepared with tamari, can be made gluten-free. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from soy sauce (~1,178 mg per serving), making it a moderate-sodium dish. The medium glycemic index (~64) is driven by the white rice base, and individuals managing blood glucose should consider lower-GI grain substitutions. Tofu provides calcium (~78 mg per 100 g), iron (~1.1 mg per 100 g), and soy isoflavones with potential cardiovascular benefits. This dish is commonly found in Western-Asian fusion cafes and health-conscious eateries across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tofu rice bowl?
Is tofu bowl suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for grilled tofu bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for firm tofu and cooked white rice with weighted averaging. GI is estimated from the white-rice-dominant composition using University of Sydney GI Database values. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation rather than direct measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for firm tofu (FDC 172443) and cooked long-grain white rice (FDC 169756), weighted by approximate dish composition (~40% tofu, ~47% rice, ~13% sauce). Direct measurement from the restaurant product would improve accuracy. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as tofu and rice contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero for this plant-based dish. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as tofu and rice are not significant sources of vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source entries. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as tofu is a plant-based food containing no dietary cholesterol. 6. The cuisine tag 'Coffee' in the input record reflects the cafe category on GrabFood Singapore and does not represent the food's actual cuisine type; reclassified as Western-Asian Fusion. 7. The food_name emoji '🌱' has been removed for canonical naming. 8. Sodium is the primary dietary concern; a single serving provides approximately 1,178 mg sodium. 9. Vegan status is false because honey is used in the sauce; dish is vegetarian. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact composite dish codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT for this specific preparation. 11. All -1 sentinel values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite estimation for this dish type; specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2), potassium_mg (115.0), calcium_mg (78.0), iron_mg (1.1), vitamin_c_mg (0.2), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (1.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.04), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.9), vitamin_b6_mg (0.07), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.3), phosphorus_mg (98.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (0.6), selenium_mcg (8.0), water_g (65.0) — all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not sentinel -1 values. No -1 sentinel values remain in the final record.
